title: Log-concave Rainbows and Where to Find Them
speaker: Chris Eur
abstract: We show that certain colored-matching numbers fit into a Lorentzian polynomial.  We achieve this via methods arising from the two featured topics of the workshop: the tropical geometry of compactifications and the convex geometry of degrees of divisors.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial rainbow-count theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a partitioned ground set, committees, panels, and the counts C_alpha, then states that their exponential generating polynomial is Lorentzian, hence has log-concavity and related one-dimensional consequences.

proof_stages.label: Projective-intersection Lorentzian template
proof_stages.summary: A general theorem is recalled: for a projective variety X of dimension d with maps to projective spaces, the exponential generating function of the corresponding general-hyperplane intersection numbers is Lorentzian.

proof_stages.label: Marked-product variety and committee maps
proof_stages.summary: To apply the geometric theorem, the speaker constructs the needed X as the graph closure of rational maps from a product of marked copies of P^1, one rational map for each committee.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological intersection target
proof_stages.summary: The desired equality is reformulated as a cup-product computation: products of the divisor classes pulled back from the committee maps should equal the panel counts C_alpha.

proof_stages.label: Multi-permutohedral fan tropicalization
proof_stages.summary: A polyhedral fan whose cones correspond to chains of committees is defined and identified, via tropical geometry, as the tropicalization of the constructed variety X.

proof_stages.label: Tropical divisors, polynomiality, and cubical volumes
proof_stages.summary: Using tropical intersection theory, real weights a_s on committees give divisor classes with polynomially varying self-intersection numbers, and in the cubical case these numbers are computed by volumes of associated polyhedral complexes.

proof_stages.label: R-multimatroid volumes and interpolation
proof_stages.summary: For special r-multimatroid parameters the polyhedral complexes are understood and their volumes equal the panel counts; polynomiality and the open intersection with the cubical cone transfer this equality to the intersection numbers needed for the original theorem.

examples.name: Three-country, three-committee triangle count
examples.note: The displayed simplex of values, especially the case alpha=(1,2,0), illustrates that C_alpha counts distinct panels obtainable rather than the number of assignments or ways to obtain them.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the d=n=3 triangle example by taking alpha=(1,2,0), listing panels that can be formed, and explaining why the count is 1 rather than the number of ways.

examples.name: Singleton non-multi special case and stellahedral fan
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the stripped-down case where all parts have one element, so the count becomes a transversal existence count and the relevant fan is stellahedral rather than multi-permutohedral.
examples.engagement_reason: In the non-multi singleton case he checks that the naive quotient R^1 by the all-one vector collapses to an uninteresting space, then identifies the stellahedral fan as the replacement.

passes.reason: the all-singleton non-multi limit collapses the quotient fan, obstructing the naive multipermutohedral construction and motivating the stellahedral replacement
The all-singleton non-multi case is a boundary case where the naive multi-permutohedral quotient collapses, showing that construction does not work and motivating the stellahedral fan.
the all-singleton non-multi specialization collapses the naive multi-permutohedral fan and forces replacement by the stellahedral fan
the all-singleton non-multi case is the minimal boundary case where the defined quotient fan collapses, obstructing the naive specialization and requiring the stellahedral fan

examples.name: Equal-cardinality cyclic-action specialization
examples.note: A question about the moduli-space origin elicits the specialization where all parts have the same cardinality, giving the cyclic symmetry present in the pointed-rational-curve motivation.
examples.engagement_reason: He only describes the equal-cardinality cyclic-action case as the original symmetry setting, without computing or applying a construction to it.

passes.reason: the equal-cardinality specialization is distinguished by its cyclic symmetry/action rather than by failure of a claim
